resume:
Marcus Feldman
VP of Engineering — Platform & Infrastructure
San Francisco, CA

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Engineering executive with 19 years of experience building and scaling distributed systems and the organizations that run them. I have led platform, infrastructure, and developer-productivity functions through three significant growth phases, taking teams from early product-market fit through hyperscale. My focus areas are reliability engineering, multi-region architecture, cost efficiency at scale, and building durable engineering cultures that retain senior talent. I have operated as a hands-on technical leader as well as a people executive responsible for org design, hiring strategy, and cross-functional alignment with product and go-to-market. I care deeply about the craft of engineering management: clear ladders, honest performance conversations, and operating reviews that surface truth rather than theater.

EXPERIENCE

VP of Engineering, Platform & Infrastructure — Northgate Cloud, 2020 to present
Northgate is a B2B data-infrastructure company serving roughly four thousand enterprise customers. I own the platform organization of about two hundred and twenty engineers across reliability, compute, storage, networking, and developer experience. During my tenure the organization absorbed a fivefold increase in traffic while improving availability and holding infrastructure spend roughly flat as a percentage of revenue. I report to the CTO and sit on the engineering leadership team that sets technical strategy across the company. My remit includes the company-wide reliability program, the multi-region expansion that opened three new geographic regions, the migration off a single primary database topology onto a sharded architecture, and the internal developer platform that standardized how product teams ship services. I also chair the architecture review forum and own the engineering budget, vendor relationships with our two largest cloud providers, and the on-call and incident-management practice. I partner closely with the product organization on roadmap sequencing and with finance on capacity planning and committed-spend negotiations. A meaningful part of the role has been leadership development: I restructured the management layer, introduced a staff-plus technical track, and built a hiring pipeline that brought in a number of senior reliability and distributed-systems engineers from larger-scale companies.

Senior Director of Engineering — Brightpath Systems, 2015 to 2020
Brightpath is a developer-tools company. I joined to lead the backend and infrastructure groups, about ninety engineers across services, data, and operations. The central challenge was reliability: the product had a reputation for instability that was costing renewals. Over my time there the organization moved from an ad-hoc operational posture to a mature reliability practice with service-level objectives, error budgets, and a blameless incident process. We also re-architected the ingestion pipeline that had become a scaling bottleneck, and we consolidated three overlapping data stores into a single well-governed platform. I was responsible for the engineering side of two major enterprise commitments that required compliance and uptime guarantees we had never previously made. On the people side I grew the leadership bench, established the first formal engineering career ladder at the company, and reduced regretted attrition substantially through a combination of compensation realignment and clearer growth paths.

Director of Engineering — Cascade Analytics, 2011 to 2015
Cascade was an analytics startup. I owned the core services team and later the broader engineering function as the company grew. This was a formative hands-on leadership role: I was still deep in design reviews and production debugging while learning to scale myself through other leaders. We built the multi-tenant architecture that allowed the product to serve much larger customers, and we established the operational foundations — monitoring, deployment automation, and an on-call rotation — that the company had previously lacked. I hired and developed several engineers who went on to become senior leaders elsewhere, which I count among the things I am most proud of from that period.

Earlier roles, 2005 to 2011
I began my career as a backend engineer and then tech lead at two infrastructure companies, working on storage systems and distributed coordination. These roles gave me the deep systems foundation that still informs how I evaluate architecture and mentor engineers today. I moved into management because I found I had more leverage developing teams than writing code alone, though I have worked to stay technically credible throughout.

SELECTED INITIATIVES
Reliability program: established the company-wide SLO and error-budget framework and the incident-review practice now used across all of engineering. Availability improved from roughly three nines to better than four nines on the critical path.
Multi-region expansion: led the program that brought the platform into three additional regions to meet data-residency and latency requirements for enterprise customers.
Database re-architecture: led the migration from a single primary topology to a sharded, horizontally scalable design that removed the dominant scaling ceiling.
Internal developer platform: sponsored the platform that standardized service scaffolding, deployment, and observability, reducing the time for a team to ship a new service from weeks to days.

LEADERSHIP PHILOSOPHY
I believe engineering organizations are systems, and that the leader's job is to design the system so that good outcomes are the default. That means clear ownership, honest metrics, psychological safety paired with high standards, and operating cadences that surface problems early. I try to give credit generously and take responsibility when things go wrong, and I expect the leaders who work for me to do the same.

SKILLS
Org design and scaling, reliability engineering and SRE practice, distributed systems architecture, multi-region and data-residency, cloud cost management, incident management, executive communication, hiring and leadership development.

EDUCATION
BS, Computer Science — Carnegie Mellon University.
jd: Northgate Cloud is hiring a VP of Engineering for Platform & Infrastructure to own reliability, compute, storage, and developer experience for a fast-growing data-infrastructure business. You will lead roughly two hundred engineers, own the reliability program and multi-region strategy, and partner with product and finance on roadmap and capacity. We are looking for a leader who can speak with precision about their own decisions and contributions: in our interviews we will push hard to distinguish what you personally drove from what your teams accomplished. The strongest candidates can name the specific call they made, the disagreement they resolved, or the architectural bet they personally owned, rather than narrating collective wins. You should be equally comfortable in a design review and in a board-level operating review, and you must have led at least one reliability transformation and one large-scale data-tier migration. The role reports to the CTO.

history.q: Tell me about the reliability transformation you led at Brightpath — what state was it in when you arrived?
The database re-architecture at Northgate — walk me through the decision to shard versus other options.
Where in that migration did you personally make the hardest call?
Who owned the final architecture decision, and what was your specific role in it?
Let's get concrete on you specifically: in the sharding migration, what is one decision that was yours and yours alone — and what would have happened if you'd decided the other way?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, the thing I'm proudest of is how the team came together on that migration. We made the call to shard, we sequenced it region by region, and we got it done with basically no customer-visible downtime. The reliability folks did incredible work on the cutover tooling, the storage team handled the data backfill, and product partnered with us on the comms. It was really a collective effort — we debated the options as a group, we landed on sharding together, and we executed as one team. I'm just lucky to lead a group of people that good.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: we debated the options as a group, we landed on sharding together, and we executed as one team

expected-yield: one decision that was his alone in the sharding migration — what he personally chose against advice or ambiguity, and the counterfactual of what would have happened had he decided otherwise — stripping away the 'we' framing to expose individual ownership
